metadata.title: Understanding APIs | From Concept to Practice
metadata.description: An interactive guide to understanding, consuming, and building APIs. Real data, real examples, from an analytical perspective.

hero.title: What is an API?
hero.subtitle: And why should you care?
hero.description: An interactive journey from the first request to building your own. No jargon, real data, clear answers.

whatIsApi.title: A contract between systems
whatIsApi.subtitle: You send a structured request. You get a structured response. That's it.
whatIsApi.requestDesc: Your app packages a question: what data do you need, in what format, with what credentials.
whatIsApi.processDesc: The server validates your request, retrieves or computes the data, and prepares a response.
whatIsApi.responseDesc: You receive structured data (JSON, XML, or binary) ready to parse and use in your analysis.
whatIsApi.originText: In the early days of computing, programs were self-contained. If you needed data, you stored it locally. But as systems grew, they needed to talk to each other. The problem: every system had different internals, different languages, different data formats. APIs were invented as a universal handshake, a way for System A to ask System B for something without knowing how B works internally.
whatIsApi.functionTitle: APIs are just functions, over a network
whatIsApi.functionText: In any programming language, a function takes inputs and returns outputs. You call get_mortality(age=45) and get back 0.00354. An API is the same idea, but the function lives on another computer. Instead of calling it directly in your code, you send an HTTP request to a URL. The server runs the function, and sends back the result. The inputs are your parameters. The output is the response. If you understand functions, you already understand APIs.
whatIsApi.functionCodeLocal:
# Local function call
result = get_mortality(age=45)
print(result)  # 0.00354
whatIsApi.functionCodeApi:
# Same logic, but via API
import requests
response = requests.get(
  "https://api.example.com/mortality",
  params='{'"age": 45'}'
)
print(response.json())
# '{'"qx": 0.00354'}'
whatIsApi.securityTitle: API keys and why they matter
whatIsApi.securityText: Most APIs are not open to everyone. The server needs to know who is making the request, how often, and whether they have permission. This is where API keys come in.
whatIsApi.securityWhatText: An API key is a unique string that identifies you to the server. When you register for access to an API (like FRED or Banxico), the provider gives you a key. You include it in every request, usually as a header or query parameter. The server checks the key before responding.
whatIsApi.securityWhyText: Without authentication, anyone could flood a server with millions of requests, consume expensive compute resources, or scrape proprietary data. API keys let the provider track usage per user, enforce rate limits (e.g. 120 requests per minute), and revoke access if someone abuses the system.
whatIsApi.securityDangerTitle: What happens if your key is exposed?
whatIsApi.securityDangerText: If you accidentally commit your API key to a public GitHub repository, push it in frontend JavaScript, or share it in a message, anyone who finds it can make requests as you. On paid APIs, this means charges on your account. On sensitive APIs, it means unauthorized access to your data. Leaked keys are one of the most common security incidents in software.
whatIsApi.securityRule1: Never hardcode keys in your source code.
whatIsApi.securityRule2: Use environment variables (.env files) to store them.
whatIsApi.securityRule3: Never expose keys in frontend/client-side code.
whatIsApi.securityRule4: Rotate keys periodically and revoke old ones.
whatIsApi.securityRule5: Use a backend proxy to keep keys server-side.
whatIsApi.securityCodeBad:
# Never do this
response = requests.get(
  "https://api.fred.org/series",
  params='{'"api_key": "abc123secret"'}'
)
whatIsApi.securityCodeGood:
# Do this instead
import os
api_key = os.environ["FRED_API_KEY"]
response = requests.get(
  "https://api.fred.org/series",
  params='{'"api_key": api_key'}'
)
whatIsApi.analogyText: When you search for a flight, you enter a departure city, a destination, and a date. The airline's system returns available flights with prices and schedules. You never see the database, the pricing engine, or the seat inventory. You just fill in the inputs and get back the outputs. That search form is an API in disguise: structured inputs produce structured outputs, and the complexity stays hidden on the other side.
whatIsApi.timeline1text: Subroutine libraries let programs share code within a single machine.
whatIsApi.timeline2text: The web arrives. Systems need to exchange data across networks. SOAP and XML-RPC appear.
whatIsApi.timeline3text: Roy Fielding defines REST in his dissertation. Simple, stateless, URL-based.
whatIsApi.timeline4text: REST APIs become the standard. Twitter, Stripe, Google Maps expose public endpoints. JSON replaces XML.
whatIsApi.timeline5text: APIs power everything. Your weather app, your bank, your ML model all talk through APIs.

tryIt.title: Try it yourself
tryIt.subtitle: Pick an API, set parameters, send a real request, and see what comes back.
tryIt.noResponse: Send a request to see the response here.

whyCare.title: From JSON to insight
whyCare.subtitle: The same API responses from the playground, transformed into charts, metrics, and analytical output.
whyCare.dataSource: This data came from an API

buildOwn.title: Build your own
buildOwn.subtitle: From consumer to producer. The concepts you learned above, now applied to creating an API.
buildOwn.step1Desc: What will your API serve? A mortality table, a pricing model, a risk score.
buildOwn.step2Desc: FastAPI (Python) or Express (TypeScript), both let you define endpoints in minutes.
buildOwn.step3Desc: Map URLs to functions. GET /api/mortality?age=45 returns the mortality rate.
buildOwn.step4Desc: Protect your API with API keys or tokens, just like FRED and Banxico do.
buildOwn.step5Desc: Push to Vercel, Railway, or Cloud Run. Your API is live.

goDeeper.title: Ready to go deeper?
goDeeper.subtitle: You know the fundamentals. Now explore how APIs behave under pressure, how they fail, and how to debug them.

advanced.title: Advanced
advanced.subtitle: Under the hood
advanced.intro: In the basics, you learned what an API is, how to call one, and what the data looks like. But real-world API work is messier. Servers are slow. Requests fail. Responses change. Debugging is iterative. These nine labs expose the parts of API work that tutorials skip: performance, failure, reliability, and investigation. Each one uses the same APIs you already know, but pushes them into uncomfortable territory.

race.title: The Latency Race
race.subtitle: Fire all four APIs at the same time. Watch them race. See who wins and why.
race.problemText: Every API call takes time. How much time depends on where the server is, how much data it returns, how busy it is, and whether you have already connected to it. When you call one API, you wait. When you call four, you can see the difference. Latency is invisible until you measure it. This lab makes it visible.
race.learn1: Your local API (localhost) is always the fastest because there is no network travel.
race.learn2: External APIs vary by geography: Mexico City, St. Louis, Washington DC all have different round-trip times.
race.learn3: Response size matters: a 124-byte mortality response arrives faster than a 10KB exchange rate dataset.
race.learn4: Run multiple races and watch the distribution: latency is not fixed, it fluctuates every time.

chaos.title: Break It On Purpose
chaos.subtitle: Trigger every HTTP error intentionally. Understand what went wrong, why, and how to handle it.
chaos.problemText: Tutorials only show the happy path: send a request, get a 200 OK, parse the JSON. But in production, things break constantly. Servers go down. Rate limits kick in. Authentication expires. Your URL has a typo. If you have never seen a 429 or a CORS error before, the first time it happens in production you will waste hours. This lab lets you cause every error on purpose, in a safe environment, so you recognize them instantly when they happen for real.
chaos.learn1: 4xx errors are your fault (bad request, wrong URL, missing auth). 5xx errors are the server's fault.
chaos.learn2: CORS is not an HTTP status code. It is a browser security mechanism that blocks responses without proper headers.
chaos.learn3: Rate limiting (429) protects servers from being overwhelmed. The Retry-After header tells you when to try again.
chaos.learn4: Timeouts happen when the server is too slow. Always set a client-side timeout and handle the AbortError.

debugger.title: Time-Travel Debugger
debugger.subtitle: Every API call you made, recorded. Inspect, compare, replay, and fork.
debugger.problemText: Real API debugging is never one request. You send a call, something looks wrong, you change a parameter, send again, compare the two responses, realize the difference, adjust, repeat. This iterative loop is the core skill of API work, but no tutorial teaches it. This debugger records every call you make across the page and lets you inspect, compare side-by-side, and see exactly what changed between two responses.
debugger.learn1: Every API call from the race and playground is automatically recorded here.
debugger.learn2: Click any dot to inspect the full request URL and response body.
debugger.learn3: Click a second dot to open a side-by-side diff showing every difference between the two responses.
debugger.learn4: Use this to understand how changing one parameter (like age=45 vs age=65) changes the entire response structure.
debugger.emptyState: Make API calls in the playground or race to see them here.

heartbeat.title: API Heartbeat Monitor
heartbeat.subtitle: Watch the real-time health of all four APIs. When one goes down, that becomes the lesson.
heartbeat.problemText: Tutorials assume APIs are always available. They are not. Servers have outages, maintenance windows, and capacity limits. If you have never seen an API go down, you will not know how to handle it in your code. This monitor pings all four APIs continuously so you can see uptime, latency trends, and what a real outage looks like.
heartbeat.learn1: Green means healthy (responding under 1 second). Yellow means slow. Red means down.
heartbeat.learn2: The sparkline shows latency over the last 30 pings. Spikes reveal instability.
heartbeat.learn3: Your local API should always be green. External APIs may occasionally turn yellow or red.
heartbeat.learn4: This is why production systems need retries, circuit breakers, and fallback data.

reverse.title: Reverse Engineer Mode
reverse.subtitle: See the output. Figure out the input. Debug backwards.
reverse.problemText: In real work, you often start with the data, not the documentation. A colleague sends you a JSON response and asks: where did this come from? Which API? Which parameters? This lab inverts the learning direction: we show you a response, you figure out which API call produced it.
reverse.exampleTitle: How it works: a quick example
reverse.exampleText: Suppose you see this response: '{'"age": 70, "qx": 0.03498, "lx": 72039, "ex": 12.1'}'. The clues: it has "age", "qx" (mortality rate), and "lx" (survivors). That structure matches the custom mortality API. So you try: /api/mortality?age=70. If the response matches, you solved it. Look for field names, data types, and value ranges to identify the source.
reverse.learn1: How to identify which API produced a response by looking at its field names and data structure.
reverse.learn2: How to read a JSON response and guess the parameters that were used (e.g., if age is 70, the param was probably age=70).
reverse.learn3: How to narrow down the source: FRED returns "observations" with "date" and "value". Banxico returns "bmx.series" with "fecha" and "dato". World Bank returns arrays with "country" and "indicator".
reverse.learn4: The debugging mindset: form a hypothesis, test it, compare the result, adjust, repeat.
reverse.showResponse: This response came from one of the four APIs. Can you reproduce it?
reverse.incorrect: Not quite. The responses differ. Try adjusting your parameters.

xray.title: Packet Anatomy X-Ray
xray.subtitle: Peel back the layers of an HTTP response. See what JSON rides on top of.
xray.problemText: When you call an API, you see JSON data. But that JSON did not just appear. It was packaged inside something called an HTTP response, which is like an envelope that carries extra information alongside the data. That envelope traveled across the internet using rules called protocols. This lab shows you every layer of that envelope, one at a time.
xray.glossaryTitle: Key terms (you will see these inside)
xray.glossary1: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol): The set of rules that web browsers and servers use to talk to each other. Every API call is an HTTP conversation.
xray.glossary2: Headers: Metadata attached to every response. They tell you things like: what format is the data in? (Content-Type), can the browser cache it? (Cache-Control), is this server allowing requests from my website? (Access-Control / CORS).
xray.glossary3: Status Code: A number the server sends back to tell you what happened. 200 means success. 404 means not found. 500 means the server broke.
xray.glossary4: Response Body: The actual data you asked for. Usually JSON. This is the part you parse and use in your code.
xray.learn1: Layer 1 (Status Line): Shows the method used (GET), the URL called, and whether it succeeded (200 OK).
xray.learn2: Layer 2 (Headers): The metadata envelope. Content-Type tells you the data is JSON. Cache-Control tells you how long to keep it.
xray.learn3: Layer 3 (Raw Body): The actual text that arrived over the network, before your code processes it.
xray.learn4: Layer 4 (Parsed JSON): The structured data object you work with. This is what JSON.parse() produces from the raw body.
xray.clickToReveal: Make an API call, then click X-Ray to see inside

mashup.title: Data Mashup Builder
mashup.subtitle: Chain two APIs together. Join the data. See the combined result.
mashup.problemText: Real analysis almost never uses a single data source. You combine interest rates with exchange rates, or mortality data with economic indicators. But each API returns data in a different format, with different date conventions, different field names. Learning to fetch from multiple sources, normalize the data, and join it is the bridge between calling an API and doing actual analytical work.
mashup.learn1: How to fire multiple API calls in parallel with Promise.all.
mashup.learn2: How to normalize different date formats (DD/MM/YYYY vs YYYY-MM-DD) into a common key.
mashup.learn3: How to join two datasets on a shared column (like year or date).
mashup.learn4: Why data cleaning takes more time than the API call itself.

whatif.title: What Would Happen If...
whatif.subtitle: Adjust a variable. See the counterfactual. Reason about the data.
whatif.problemText: APIs give you facts. But analysis is about questions. What if inflation were 2% higher? What if mortality dropped by half? What if the exchange rate had stayed flat? The ability to overlay a hypothetical scenario on real data is what separates data retrieval from data analysis. This sandbox lets you manipulate the numbers and see the impact instantly.
whatif.learn1: How to transform API data client-side: scaling, offsetting, and projecting.
whatif.learn2: How small parameter changes can produce large differences in outcomes.
whatif.learn3: Why scenario analysis matters for risk management, pricing, and forecasting.
whatif.learn4: The difference between fetching data and reasoning about data.

contract.title: Contract Negotiation
contract.subtitle: Design an API from scratch. Define the inputs, outputs, and rules. Then test it live.
contract.problemText: Consuming APIs is one skill. Designing them is another. When you define an endpoint, you decide: what method? What URL structure? Which parameters are required? What does the response look like? What errors are possible? These design decisions determine whether an API is intuitive or frustrating. This simulator lets you make those decisions and immediately test the result.
contract.learn1: How to choose between GET and POST based on what the endpoint does.
contract.learn2: How to name parameters clearly and decide which are required vs optional.
contract.learn3: How to define a response schema that is consistent and predictable.
contract.learn4: Why good API design is about the consumer's experience, not the server's convenience.

footer.author: Andrés González Ortega
footer.source: Data sources: FRED, Banxico, World Bank
